Someone spent months turning Claude Code into an actual operati...
rawContent:
Tweet by @Suryanshti777 (Suryansh Tiwari)
Author bio: Exploring AI & SaaS trends early
Sharing what’s actually useful Helping builders turn ideas → products → traction – 📩 Open to collabs
Text: This is wild 🤯

Someone spent months turning Claude Code into an actual operating system for AI agents.

Not a prompt pack.
Not a “10x coding workflow.”
Not another repo full of copied system prompts.

ECC feels like the point where AI coding stops being “chatting with a model” and starts looking like real infrastructure.

246 skills.
61 agents.
Memory systems.
Verification loops.
Security layers.
Multi-agent orchestration.
Cross-platform workflows.
Hooks.
Eval harnesses.
Context recovery.
Continuous learning.

And the craziest part is how obsessive the whole thing feels.

You can literally see thousands of hours of real usage baked into it.

Every command. Every workflow. Every edge case. Every recovery system.

Most AI repos look like demos.

ECC looks like someone got tired of AI agents breaking in production and decided to build the missing layer themselves.

182k+ stars suddenly makes a lot of sense.

Most people still think Claude Code is the product.

The real power is everything people are building around it.

ECC is one of the first projects that genuinely feels like a full operating layer for AI agents instead of “better prompts.”

It turns Claude Code into something way bigger: persistent memory, parallel agents, verification systems, security audits, workflow orchestration, self-improving skills, cross-harness execution.

This is the kind of repo that changes how serious builders use AI coding tools.

Not because it looks flashy.

Because it solves the painful stuff everyone hits after the demo works.

Someone basically looked at Claude Code and said:

“what if this thing behaved like a real software system instead of a chat interface?”

And then built ECC.

The amount of depth in this repo is honestly ridiculous.

Agents. Skills. Hooks. Memory persistence. Eval systems. Security tooling. MCP integrations. Multi-agent workflows. Cross-platform support. Continuous learning.

This isn’t “AI Twitter productivity.”

This is infrastructure.

The scary part?

Most people won’t understand why this matters yet.

But the people building serious agent workflows absolutely will.
